summary: Julia Evans frames women-only tech spaces as a programming 'hack' — a quick fix that alleviates symptoms without addressing the root architectural problem. She explains that the core bug is women being treated badly at tech meetups, and excluding men is a simple way to prevent harassment. She acknowledges the downsides, including excluding allies and creating complexity around inclusion of trans and nonbinary people. She draws on her experience organizing Montreal All-Girl Hack Night and contrasts women-only spaces with alternative approaches like the Recurse Center's community-building model. The post argues these spaces aren't ideal but are pragmatically effective at making people feel safer.
key_points: Women-only spaces are a pragmatic workaround for the deeper problem of women being treated badly at tech events
The hack has real downsides: excluding allies, requiring careful decisions about trans and nonbinary inclusion
Alternative approaches exist, like the Recurse Center's strong community norms and selective admission, or Boston Python Workshop's invitation model
The decision to keep Montreal All-Girl Hack Night women-only was driven by attendees who said they felt safer that way
money_quotes: Imagine you have a program, and it has a pretty serious issue. It needs some deep architectural changes to fix it, but you can alleviate some of the symptoms by just changing a few lines of code.
If there are no men, nobody can get harassed by men. That's it. That's the entire hack.
This has the, to me, unpleasant side effect of excluding lots of people who I would like to hang out with.
The goal is to have a fun technical meetup where nobody gets asked if they're 'really a programmer' because they're wearing makeup.
We're not trying to change the world. We're just trying to learn some things, meet some people, and have a good time.
Women-only events aren't a perfect solution, but they can be effective at making people feel safer. It's a hack and it doesn't fix the root cause of the bug, but sometimes it's good enough for now.
key_insight: Women-only tech spaces are an imperfect but pragmatically effective workaround for harassment — a hack that alleviates symptoms while the deeper architectural problem remains unsolved.
